chunk_summary: Guidelines for preventing refeeding syndrome in children with neurodisability, focusing on risk screening, conservative calorie advancement, electrolyte monitoring, and caregiver counseling.
learning_objectives: Understand the risk factors and clinical markers of refeeding syndrome in children with neurodisability.
Learn the operational protocol for safe calorie advancement, including baseline assessments and electrolyte monitoring.
Recognize the importance of caregiver counseling and follow-up in preventing complications during refeeding.
key_facts: Refeeding syndrome risk is highest in children with recent rapid weight loss, low BMI-for-age, or abnormal baseline electrolytes.
Initial calorie intake for high-risk children should be 40–50% of the calculated goal, with gradual advancement over several days.
Baseline labs should include phosphorus, potassium, magnesium, and glucose, with daily monitoring during early refeeding.
Hypophosphatemia is the classic marker of refeeding syndrome, but potassium and magnesium shifts and glucose instability are also critical.
Thiamine supplementation should be considered in high-risk cases.
Clinical warning signs include lethargy, vomiting, tachycardia, fluid overload, and increased respiratory effort.
reference_quote: "Start nutrition conservatively in high-risk children (commonly around 40–50% of calculated goal) and advance gradually over several days while monitoring electrolytes daily early in the course." (ASPEN consensus recommendations for refeeding syndrome)
document_key_topics: Refeeding syndrome risk factors and markers
